generic.definition: The geological deposit type you are exploring for. Controls which scoring model and thresholds are used.
generic.why: Different deposit types have fundamentally different host rocks, alteration patterns, and geophysical signatures. Choosing the correct type is the single most important setting.

generic.definition: Rock code for the primary structural control (fault, contact, or vein-hosting unit).
generic.why: Used to compute the C2 structural halo score — cells near this rock type score higher.
generic.range: Any integer from your rock code mapping

SEDEX Pb-Zn.definition: Code for the structural marker controlling fold-axis ore localisation.
SEDEX Pb-Zn.example: At Kayad (Rajasthan): code 3 = Pegmatite, which tracks the fold hinge. At Rampura-Agucha: pegmatitic dykes serve the same role.
SEDEX Pb-Zn.suggestion: If your SEDEX has a different structural marker (graphitic schist, carbonate bed), update rock_codes and use that integer.

VMS Cu-Zn.definition: In VMS systems, typically the stringer zone or felsic-mafic contact marking the feeder pipe.
VMS Cu-Zn.example: At Kidd Creek (Ontario): rhyolite-basalt contact. At Neves-Corvo (Portugal): volcaniclastic unit. At Greens Creek (Alaska): stringer zone.
VMS Cu-Zn.suggestion: Choose the code for the rock type defining the hydrothermal upflow zone in your deposit.

Epithermal Au.definition: In epithermal deposits, typically the silicified zone or main fault/vein lithology.
Epithermal Au.example: At Hishikari (Japan): banded quartz vein material. At Waihi (New Zealand): Martha Fault silicified andesite.
Epithermal Au.suggestion: Use the rock code for your dominant vein host or silicified alteration package.

Porphyry Cu-Mo.definition: Typically the causative mineralised porphyry intrusive body itself.
Porphyry Cu-Mo.example: At El Teniente (Chile): quartz-monzonite porphyry. At Chuquicamata (Chile): porphyry stocks. At Grasberg (Indonesia): diorite porphyry.
Porphyry Cu-Mo.suggestion: Use the code for the productive intrusive body, not the barren host rocks.

generic.definition: Rock code for the footwall unit below the ore horizon (if applicable).
generic.why: Used to compute C3 footwall standoff score — distance from this contact affects prospectivity.

SEDEX Pb-Zn.definition: The footwall horizon. At Kayad, this is Calc-Silicate Rock (CSR) which sits below the ore.
SEDEX Pb-Zn.example: Kayad: CSR forms a distinctive basement to the mineralisation. At Mt Isa: graphitic siltstone.
SEDEX Pb-Zn.suggestion: Standoff of 10-40m above the footwall is optimal for SEDEX ore.

VMS Cu-Zn.definition: For VMS, often not a distinct footwall — the alteration pipe itself is the footwall zone.
VMS Cu-Zn.suggestion: Consider disabling this criterion or mapping to the alteration pipe rock code.

generic.definition: Distance breakpoints (metres) for the structural marker halo score.
generic.why: Defines how far from the structural control mineralisation is expected. Closer bands score higher.

SEDEX Pb-Zn.definition: PG (Pegmatite) halo distance breaks. Peak mineralisation occurs 4-10m inboard from the contact.
SEDEX Pb-Zn.example: Kayad: 4-10m from PG contact = highest ore density. 10-15m is still productive. >30m rarely economic.

Porphyry Cu-Mo.definition: Distance from intrusive body margin. Porphyry mineralisation is broader and deeper than SEDEX.
Porphyry Cu-Mo.example: Typical porphyry envelope: 100-500m around stock. Use [50, 100, 250, 500, 800, 1200, 2000] or similar.

generic.definition: Distance breaks (metres) for footwall standoff scoring in the upper regime.
generic.why: In the upper mine, ore forms at a distance above the footwall — close OR far is unfavourable.

SEDEX Pb-Zn.definition: CSR standoff — distance above the Calc-Silicate Rock footwall.
SEDEX Pb-Zn.example: Kayad upper mine: 10-40m standoff is optimal. Below 10m or above 60m, less ore.

generic.definition: Distance breaks (metres) from the ore shoot plunge axis.
generic.why: C7 plunge proximity — ore follows a preferred plunge vector. Cells near this vector score higher.

SEDEX Pb-Zn.definition: Distance from the Kayad N28°E (upper) or N315°E (lower) ore shoot axes.
SEDEX Pb-Zn.example: Shallow shoot: 30°/075°E plunge, shifts 122m east per 100m down. Deep shoot: 12°/063°E, shifts 410m east per 100m down.

Porphyry Cu-Mo.definition: Distance from the deposit centre (usually the intrusive centroid).
Porphyry Cu-Mo.example: Use larger distances — porphyry systems extend kilometres. Try [100, 300, 600, 1000].

Epithermal Au.definition: Distance from the controlling fault or vein array centroid.

generic.definition: Minimum distance from known ore (metres) for a cell to be considered 'novel' (potential greenfield target).
generic.why: Used by the blind/environment model — novelty bonus applied beyond this distance.
generic.range: 100 to 5000 m depending on deposit scale

SEDEX Pb-Zn.example: Kayad: 500m threshold identifies cells outside known ore lenses as 'blind' candidates.

Porphyry Cu-Mo.example: Use larger (1000-2000m) — porphyry systems are large; close-in targets are extensions, not blind targets.

generic.definition: Rock code for the 'never ore-bearing' lithology. Cells with this code have scores capped at veto_score_cap regardless of other criteria.
generic.why: Prevents false positives in known barren host rocks.

SEDEX Pb-Zn.example: Kayad: Amphibolite (code 2) is the flanking wall rock and is never mineralised. Scores are capped at 20 regardless of gravity/magnetic response.

generic.definition: Maximum score (0-100) allowed in veto-coded cells.
generic.why: A hard ceiling prevents false-positive prospectivity in known-barren rocks.

generic.definition: Magnetic susceptibility breakpoints (μSI) for absolute scoring.
generic.why: Many deposits show characteristic magnetic signatures (lows or highs).

SEDEX Pb-Zn.definition: Kayad ore shows a persistent local magnetic minimum within a broadly positive field.
SEDEX Pb-Zn.example: Values below -10 μSI are prospective (coincident with ore). Values above 30 are typically barren amphibolite.

Porphyry Cu-Mo.example: Porphyry phyllic alteration destroys magnetite → magnetic low. Potassic alteration forms magnetite → magnetic high. Central phyllic zone scores high with negative breaks.

generic.definition: Gravity breakpoints (mGal) for absolute scoring in the upper depth regime.
generic.why: Dense orebodies create local gravity anomalies. The sign depends on the deposit.

SEDEX Pb-Zn.definition: Kayad ore is density-NEGATIVE relative to flanking amphibolite — unusual!
SEDEX Pb-Zn.example: -0.03 to -0.19 mGal = strong ore signal. Values above 0.30 = flanking dense amphibolite = score low.
SEDEX Pb-Zn.suggestion: Counter-intuitive: here, gravity LOWS are prospective, not highs. Verify your deposit's density contrast before using defaults.

Porphyry Cu-Mo.example: Porphyry systems often produce gravity highs from mineralisation + potassic alteration. Use positive breaks.
